title: Review: Questlove’s Earth, Wind & Fire Doc Is a Must-see

blocks.content: The Black Panthers were under government attack and the civil-rights movement was in peril following the murders of MLK Jr. and Malcolm X, when Memphis singer, kalimba player, and prolific session drummer Maurice White had a vision for his own band. Earth, Wind & Fire, a project named after his astrological reading, coalesced in the late ’60s, blending soul, funk, Latin jazz, rock, and more. By the 1970s they were one of the biggest groups in America, arguably on Earth. Their reach was owed not just to the nine-member unit’s technical excellence and versatility but also their endless supply of positivity, of psychedelic Black power characterized by its leader’s unfailing belief in a beautiful tomorrow. The band invited listeners to an intergalactic fantasy, and so Questlove’s must-see new HBO documentary Earth, Wind & Fire (To Be Celestial vs That’s the Weight of the World), the hip-hop veteran’s fourth after Sly Lives! (aka the Burden of Black Genius), immerses viewers in his subject’s cosmic inclinations. You don’t just hear about Maurice White as a transcendental meditation practitioner who claimed he was abducted by aliens and succeeded at astral projection. You slip into mindfulness routines and affirmations. The film is a refreshing retreat to a bygone world of mid-20th-century spirituality; it’s also a sometimes painfully honest primer on the stories behind and in between the band’s biggest songs. Quest’s latest doesn’t just outline the rise, fall, and resurgence of a trusty if not universally deeply known pop-culture tenet, like most talking-head music docs. It fluctuates seamlessly between profundity and absurdity the same way the late White is said to have done.

Earth, Wind & Fire aims to leave you with a feel for the era it spends much of its time with, nodding to the minimalism of New Age music and spoken word records. Between needle drops of Earth, Wind & Fire classics, the Roots deliver a floaty, ambient score favoring the kind of muted groove heard on self-help recordings in the group’s heyday, drenching the film in sounds that might have accompanied White in his deep space musing. This documentary is, like clips of Soul Train dancers electrified by the group’s hits, aggressively ’70s. The 55-year-old Roots co-founder and drummer has stitched together a biography of the Earth, Wind & Fire founder and drummer that’s dripping in the gold-toned wonderment of having connected with White’s life and music as a child. As the subtitle suggests, Quest sees Maurice White’s yearning eccentricity as essential to his triumphs but just as instrumental to a distance his family and bandmates could feel. He frames White’s story as an intricate struggle between devoting himself to the masses, to his compatriots, to his significant other and children, and to drug-free mind expansion. There aren’t enough hours in the day for everything, and the doc’s survey of the band’s sweeping lineup changes offers serious contrasts to the good feelings forever associated with White’s catalogue. Earth, Wind & Fire appears to confirm that the 21st of “September” that gets memed each autumn is actually White’s first son’s projected due date, but we also learn that this same son had difficulty navigating a relationship with a father who was so intensely committed to his work.

Quest approaches this story as a musician who famously helped D’Angelo shape 2000’s Voodoo by watching countless hours of soul and funk band routines. He once again knows what footage to pull. He makes sure we have eyes on California Jam 1974, the televised folk and rock festival where the funk titans won over a quarter-million Black Sabbath and Seals & Crofts fans with a balance of theatrical silliness and virtuosity. Quest nets Barack Obama, the first voice heard in the film, who talks about being blown away by EWF tours. He also gets Stevie Wonder to admit that the opening piano line in “I Wish” is his take on the guitar lick that kicks off 1975’s “Shining Star.” Earth, Wind & Fire isn’t nerdy so much as accessibly professorial: It explains how an engineer can tame nine musicians by letting you hear “Shining Star” both as a cacophony of unmixed instrument tracks and then as a tightly balanced single. It outlines the job of a funk rhythm guitarist by reinforcing the difference in percussive punch between soulcentric early gems and late-’70s thumpers. The doc doesn’t get bogged down in details or gloss entirely over the technicalities musicians often talk among themselves about; it wants you just as obsessed with EWF’s story as the director is and feeds you the technical tidbits you’d need to know to get the whole picture. Quest sets about filling casual admirers in on the birth of a sound that was inspired by White’s travels through North and South America and Africa; on the lore of cuts like 1975’s “Reasons,” a wedding-reception staple that singer Philip Bailey stresses is actually about a one-night stand; and on the group’s increasingly extravagant gigs.

But Earth, Wind & Fire doesn’t, in its reverence for the music and players, slouch on exploring the Weight portion of the subtitle. The film’s back end tallies the literal and figurative costs of ambition. Not long after revisiting the era in the late ’70s when bassist (and brother of Maurice) Verdine White would be lifted into the air during a solo without missing notes, the doc adds that touring with that much pyrotechnics and magic tricks made the band a money-losing venture. Earth, Wind & Fire loves the story it’s telling enough to believe the group’s mistakes are as much the stuff of legend as their ascent, so it stews in post-peak drama like consequential departures and firings: It notes that Phil Collins poached White’s horn section for 1981’s Face Value and scored a hit in 1984’s “Easy Lover” with Bailey, who’d just been let go from EWF. White is portrayed as an inspiration to the world and a loner who pushed his associates away until he needed them. The saga of Earth, Wind & Fire is a microcosm of the spirit of the ’70s — where excess, abstract spirituality, and colorful futurism assuaged anxieties about the grubby present — that is vital viewing today. As in the group’s mid-to-late-’70s heyday, we approach another American national anniversary in need of a jolt of their infectious optimism. Speaking at Earth, Wind & Fire’s Tribeca Film Festival premiere, Quest said he hoped his doc would take people’s minds off today’s toxicity (though he didn’t get specific). After the film, he manned a drum kit to back Earth, Wind & Fire for a performance. For a little while it looked like playing behind Verdine, Bailey, and longtime singer/percussionist Ralph Johnson on cuts like “September” was the position Quest had been waiting his whole life to saunter into — not his current hot streak as our big-screen Black pop-culture archivist in residence.
